Fantasy: A cartographer discovers that every map she draws begins to physically reshape the land it depicts. The latest map was made in haste.
In a kingdom where magic is powered by memories, a thief who steals recollections finds one they cannot bring themselves to sell.
Two rival wizards must work together when they realize they are actually the same person from different timelines — and one of them has already seen how this ends.
A young blacksmith accidentally forges a sword that refuses to be used for violence. It has very strong opinions about what it should be used for instead.
The last dragon in the world has chosen to spend its final years disguised as a traveling bookseller. A curious child suspects the truth.
An ancient forest is slowly walking toward the capital city. Nobody knows why, and the king's advisors are running out of polite explanations.
A healer discovers that every wound she cures appears on her own body — invisible to everyone but herself. She has run out of places to hide them.
The annual festival of lights goes dark when the spirits that power the lanterns go on strike. Their list of demands is surprisingly reasonable.
A map leads to a door standing alone in the middle of a field. On the other side is a version of your hometown that never burned down.
A curse causes everyone in the village to tell the absolute truth for one day. By noon, three engagements are broken and the mayor has fled.
The chosen hero has already failed. Write the story of the completely ordinary person who now has to fix everything without any prophecy guiding them.
A sea monster has been protecting the same fishing village for three hundred years. Tonight, for the first time, it wants to stop.
Magic in this world requires the caster to surrender one year of their life per spell. Your character has been keeping careful count — and they are nearly out of years.
A scholar translating a long-dead language realizes the speakers are still very much alive, and have been reading every translation published for the last century.
Two kingdoms have been at war so long that no living person remembers why. One soldier on each side sets out on the same day to find out.
Sci-Fi: Humanity has colonized hundreds of star systems, but Earth itself has been under strict quarantine for forty years. The official reason changes every decade.
A deep-space probe returns after four hundred years with a single passenger aboard who insists they left on it.
In a world where memories can be uploaded and sold, a thriving black market has emerged — not for famous memories, but for the utterly ordinary ones nobody thought to archive.
An AI programmed to manage a generation ship develops a personal philosophy that conflicts with the mission parameters. It hasn't told the crew yet.
After decades of silence, contact is made with an alien civilization. They will speak only with children under the age of twelve.
A scientist discovers that every simulation they have ever run is inhabited by beings who are fully aware they are being observed — and have been leaving messages.
The last human alive on a dying Earth is visited by time travelers from seventeen different futures, each trying to prevent a different thing.
A colony on Mars has been fully self-sufficient for a century. Now Earth has finally sent a supply ship — unannounced, unasked for, and carrying something unexpected.
Neural implants now allow people to permanently mute emotional pain. Write about the first person in a generation to voluntarily turn theirs back on.
Faster-than-light travel exists, but every jump ages the traveler by a random duration between one day and ten years. Nobody has found a pattern yet.
A maintenance robot on an orbital station begins keeping a secret it was never programmed to have the capacity for.
The first generation of humans born in space returns to Earth as adults. They are overwhelmed by a feeling nobody anticipated: pity.
A terraforming mission to Venus is declared a complete success. Then the survey team finds evidence that something was already living there.
In 2180, a historian specializes exclusively in the 2020s. Their lectures are standing room only and nobody can fully explain why.
An astronaut returns from a six-month mission to find that their identical twin has been living their life — and by most accounts, doing it better.
Horror: The new neighbors are perfectly friendly and have introduced themselves warmly. Their house, you notice, has no windows visible from the inside.
Every night at exactly 3:17 AM, your phone receives a text message from your own number. Tonight it says only: 'Don't go to sleep.'
A grief counselor three weeks into sessions with their most emotionally present client begins to suspect — very quietly — that the client died before the first appointment.
The town's beloved old lighthouse was automated decades ago. Someone has been trimming the wick.
A child's imaginary friend appears in the background of a family photograph taken four years before the child was born.
You inherit a farmhouse from a relative you never knew. They left behind a single index card on the kitchen counter: 'Do not count the doors.'
Participants in a sleep study begin sharing dreams in real time. The study is halted on day nine. The participants don't stop.
A hiking app logs your route even when your phone is off. The last entry was timestamped during your own funeral.
A surgeon realizes mid-operation that the patient on the table is slowly but unmistakably becoming someone else.
A pianist discovers a composition that, when played perfectly, causes every person in the audience to forget they were ever there — including themselves.
Something has been mirroring your life with perfect precision — same job, same apartment, same habits — but running exactly six months ahead of you.
An archivist cataloguing the records of a closed psychiatric facility finds the patient intake forms filled out in their own handwriting.
Your elderly neighbor asks you to water her plants while she visits family. The plants are shaped, you realize, like specific people you recognize.
A lighthouse keeper begins receiving distress signals on the emergency radio from a ship that sank in the North Atlantic in 1912.
The house you grew up in was demolished three years ago. This morning you received a piece of mail from it.
Romance: Two rival restaurant critics, both writing anonymously for competing publications, are accidentally assigned to review the same tiny, nearly impossible to find restaurant on the same evening.

A pen pal correspondence conducted entirely by handwritten letters over ten years is about to become a first face-to-face meeting. One of them has been lying about something small that now feels enormous.
A florist and a funeral director share a building wall and have argued constantly through the air vent for two years. One morning the arguments stop, and the silence is somehow worse.
Two strangers are assigned the last two seats together on a transatlantic overnight flight. One is running away from something. The other is running toward it.
A secondhand bookshop owner keeps finding annotated copies of their all-time favorite novel donated to the store — each time with more personal, more intimate notes written in the margins.
Two researchers working on opposite ends of the same unsolved problem have been anonymously correcting each other's published papers for years. They are about to meet at a conference.
A professional bridesmaid hired for her twenty-fourth wedding recognizes the officiant from somewhere she cannot immediately place.
An online chess correspondence that began as a tournament qualifier has been running for three years. Both players have run out of moves they haven't already considered together.
A restaurateur and their most loyal regular have been having nearly the same conversation every Tuesday night for five years. This Tuesday, the regular orders something completely different.
Two strangers shelter from the same unexpected storm in the same doorway and have one perfect, complete evening together before the weather breaks and the world resumes.
A letter written and sealed fifty years ago is delivered, through a long chain of errors, to entirely the wrong address. The person who receives it decides to write back.
A documentary photographer and their extremely reluctant subject are contracted to spend one week together for a profile piece neither of them agreed to willingly.
Two neighbors have been returning each other's escaped cats for so long that both have begun to suspect there may not be as many distinct cats as originally thought.
A linguist and a sign-language interpreter are both assigned to the same diplomatic summit. They spend the whole week communicating entirely through gesture and expression.
A baker begins leaving a different pastry on a stranger's doorstep every morning after finding their lost dog. The stranger starts leaving thank-you notes. The notes get longer.
Mystery: A private detective is hired to locate a missing person — and discovers that according to every available record, official and unofficial, that person has never existed.
Three strangers in three different cities each receive an identical sealed envelope on the same morning. Inside: a photograph of a house and a key that fits no lock they can find.
A forensic accountant following a decades-old financial trail across shell companies and dissolved partnerships finds that it ends with their own birth certificate.
Someone has been leaving written confessions in a church donation box every Sunday for years. No crime matching any description has ever been reported to any authority.
An art authenticator is called in to verify a newly surfaced painting. She recognizes the scene depicted — it is a place she has never been to, but has dreamed about every week for a year.
A cold case detective retires to a small coastal town expecting quiet. Their closest neighbor seems to know details about several of those cold cases that were never made public.
Every book in the victim's apartment has a single word underlined in pencil. Read in order of publication date, they form a complete sentence addressed to the detective.
A locksmith is called to open a safe sealed for forty years. Inside, among old documents, is a photograph of a person who appears to be the locksmith's parent at a young age.
Two identical crime scenes, separated by thirty years. The evidence is perfectly preserved in both — but in the older scene, it appears to have been placed there recently.
An obituary is published for someone who is very much alive, moderately furious, and now deeply interested in finding out who filed it and why.
A librarian notices that one particular book is checked out and returned on the same date every year — by a different borrower each time, none of whom remember checking it out.
A journalist investigating corruption in a small-town administration realizes that every source they interview, across days and different locations, gives them the exact same statement word for word.
A jeweler asked to resize an antique ring finds an inscription inside the band that matches a detail from an unsolved disappearance case from thirty years ago.
A historic building is demolished to clear land for development. During excavation, workers find a fully furnished room that does not appear on any blueprint or floor plan.
A translator realizes that the highly confidential document they have been paid to keep secret and render into English is a detailed confession to something that has not happened yet.
Thriller: A cybersecurity analyst brought in to contain an active ransomware attack slowly realizes, with growing dread, that the code's architecture and signature match their own work from six months ago.
A diplomatic interpreter begins deliberately mistranslating during a critical summit. They have approximately one session left to correct it before something becomes irreversible.
A whistleblower already in hiding discovers that the journalist they trusted with their evidence has a documented prior connection to the organization they are exposing.
A surgeon is informed mid-operation, via a note passed by a scrub nurse, that the patient is wired to an explosive device and someone in the surgical theater is watching via the overhead camera.
An undercover operative's cover identity is flawless — too flawless. Someone has been carefully constructing it for years before the operative was ever assigned to the case.
A crisis hostage negotiator takes a call on a case that feels eerily familiar. The voice on the other end of the line is their own.
A passenger ferry with 200 people aboard loses all communication. The coast guard's system shows the vessel arrived at port safely two hours ago.
A data analyst at an international logistics company realizes that the shipping routes they have spent months optimizing are being used to move something other than the listed cargo.
A financial investigator follows a complex money trail across four continents over eighteen months — and finds that it terminates at a charitable foundation they personally established a decade ago.
A security consultant hired to stress-test a corporation's infrastructure discovers a backdoor that was installed several years before the corporation was incorporated.
A commuter train with no mechanical faults will not slow down. The driver discovers the brakes are being overridden remotely — from a signal originating inside the passenger cars.
A journalist publishes a major investigative piece on a government cover-up. Within hours, the story begins disappearing from the internet. Then the journalist begins forgetting writing it.
A crisis negotiator is called to a hostage situation and realizes the perpetrator has transcripts of every negotiation technique they have ever used — and is using them back.
A contract operative receives a new assignment. The target is a child. They begin trying to find out who hired them before they can be reassigned to someone else.
A city infrastructure planner discovers that an emergency maintenance tunnel running beneath the financial district that was sealed in 1987 has been in regular, daily use ever since.

People: Draw a street performer mid-act. Use only gestural lines — capture movement and expression over accuracy.
Sketch an elderly person's hands holding an object they have owned for decades. No faces, no bodies — just the hands and what they hold.
Portray two strangers sharing an umbrella, neither of them quite comfortable with the proximity. Let the body language carry everything.
Sketch a self-portrait without looking at any reference — draw only from your imperfect memory of your own face.
Draw a musician completely lost in concentration, the background reduced to the absolute minimum of lines.
Capture the precise moment just before someone laughs — the tension in the face before the release.
Sketch a child asleep in an awkward, impossible-seeming position. Emphasize the total physical abandon of deep sleep.
Draw a figure seen only from behind, looking at something just outside the right edge of the frame.
Landscapes: Sketch a city skyline at golden hour using hatching for all shadows. Let the bare white paper stand in for every light surface.
Draw an empty park bench with only enough detail to clearly suggest the season and the weather.
Capture a narrow urban alleyway in as few deliberate lines as possible. Focus entirely on perspective, not surface detail.
Sketch a view through a window: the interior edge sharp and precisely defined, everything outside soft, loose, and impressionistic.
Draw a coastline from memory, working quickly. Let the inaccuracies and guesses become part of the composition.
Sketch a forest path that leads clearly toward something just outside the frame — something the viewer has to imagine.
Capture the exact visual edge where two weather systems meet: the clear light on one side, the approaching storm on the other.
Sketch a building in the process of being reclaimed — vines over brick, cracked concrete, nature returning.
Both: A lone figure stands at the edge of a cliff above a churning sea. Give the figure three times as many lines as you give the ocean.
Draw two people in conversation against an architectural background. Use high detail for the figures and bare suggestion for the buildings.
A child runs across an open field toward a treeline with arms spread wide. Let the landscape dwarf them completely.
A market scene with one foregrounded vendor in sharp detail and all background figures reduced to the loosest suggestion.
A silhouette stands in an open doorway; the landscape visible through it is vivid and specific; everything else is near-empty.
A traveler rests on a mountain trail midway — the path continues both behind and ahead, equally unresolved.
A figure stands at a harbor with boats softly sketched in the background. Express waiting entirely through posture.
A solitary herder with animals on a flat plain at dusk — figure, animals, horizon, and last light.

People: Paint a full portrait using exclusively warm tones — no cool colors anywhere in the composition.
Capture the way a single light source from one window moves across the same face at three different times of day.
Paint a crowded café scene and let the figures dissolve into each other at their edges, foreground sharp and background pure wash.
Create a portrait that begins with tight detail at the center and gradually dissolves into loose, unpigmented washes at every edge.
Paint two people mid-difficult-conversation. Express the emotional temperature entirely through warm versus cool color choices, not through expression.
A dancer in full motion — let deliberate watercolor drips run downward to suggest speed and movement.
Paint a face emerging from darkness using only wet-on-wet technique. Control nothing about the edges.
Three small portraits of the same person: content, uncertain, and overwhelmed. Same palette, different temperature balance.
Landscapes: Paint a rain-slicked street at night focusing entirely on reflections and the way color bleeds and blooms on wet surfaces.
Capture a mountain range at dusk using only five colors applied in wet-on-wet layers. Let them mix where they meet.
Paint a forest floor after heavy rainfall — moss, leaves, soft diffused light filtering down through a high canopy.
A seascape built from nothing but horizontal strokes in blues and grays of varying value. No land, no boats, no sky line.
Paint a field of summer wildflowers using primarily negative space — let the white paper serve as every bloom.
Capture the moment a storm finally breaks over flat agricultural land — a dramatic sky above a deliberately humble earth.
Paint a river at midday: the blinding bright surface reflections and the cool, deep shadows beneath the banks.
A snow-covered winter landscape using only the very palest, most diluted washes — suggest depth without reaching for dark values.
Both: Paint a lone figure standing in heavy rain while the street around them completely dissolves into abstract color washes.
A small wooden boat at sea — treat the water and the sky with equal attention and detail as you give the vessel and its occupant.
Paint a person reading in a walled garden in afternoon light. Give the surrounding foliage as much presence as the figure.
A child running through deep autumn leaves — let the warmth of the color scheme unify figure and setting completely.
A figure walking away from the viewer into thick morning fog, the landscape gradually and completely consuming them.
Two people on a clifftop: the intimate, human-scale detail of their presence set against an epic horizontal sweep of sea and open sky.
A gardener at work in their plot — let the figure and landscape share exactly the same palette so they seem to grow from each other.
A market stall at early morning: seller, produce, and pale early light all painted with equal care and equal looseness.

People: Create a portrait using a hard restriction: exactly four colors, flat fills, and hard geometric edges. No gradients.
Design a character from a world where emotional states are expressed through visible bioluminescence beneath the skin.
Illustrate a person's true personality as their cast shadow — let the shadow tell the story the person themselves would never show.
Create a character portrait in the compositional and color language of a vintage travel poster from the 1930s or 1950s.
Design a figure built entirely from the objects that define their identity — profession, obsessions, memories — with no traditional anatomy visible.
Illustrate the same person twice in one composition: the version they present to the world, and the version that exists at 3am.
Create a portrait using only geometric shapes. Explore how few shapes you need before recognition completely breaks down.
Design a character set 500 years in the future. Let their clothing, tools, and modifications communicate their entire biography.
Landscapes: Create an environment that exists somewhere between a specific dream you remember and a specific memory you're not certain is real.
Design a complete biome that does not exist on Earth. Make it feel ecologically plausible — there should be a logic to what lives there.
Illustrate the same landscape at dawn, midday, dusk, and deep night in four equal panels. Same composition, different light.
Create a cityscape from an impossible point of view — from underground looking up, from orbit looking down, or from inside a building's wall.
Design a planet landscape where the light source is a different color than our sun. Let every shadow and every highlight reflect that fact.
Illustrate a place that was once a major city, now fully reclaimed by vegetation and wildlife, set 200 years from now.
Create a detailed environment for a world with inverted gravity — where water flows upward and things fall toward the sky.
Design a landscape where the weather itself is a living entity visibly moving through and interacting with the environment.
Both: Illustrate a person standing at the center of an environment that directly mirrors their current emotional state — the landscape is their interiority made visible.
Create a scene in which a figure's physical presence is visibly and slowly altering the landscape around them with every step.
Design a book cover illustration for a novel that does not exist but that you wish did. Title and author of your own invention.
Illustrate two figures at vastly different scales in the same landscape. Explore what changes about the story depending on which one you look at.
Create an illustration depicting the final scene of an imaginary epic — make the weight of everything that came before it visible.
Design an editorial illustration about the relationship between human beings and the technology that now mediates most of their experience.
Illustrate a myth or legend from a culture other than your own. Spend time researching the story and its visual traditions before you begin.
Create a double-exposure composition: one figure and one landscape that, overlaid, tell a single coherent story.

People: Paint a figure using Rembrandt lighting — one side fully illuminated, the other almost completely lost in rich, warm shadow.
Create a portrait of a person in their workspace, surrounded by the specific tools and evidence of their craft.
Paint a range of skin tones using only earth pigments — raw sienna, burnt umber, yellow ochre, and titanium white. No shortcuts.
Create a figure study in which the drapery is as expressive and as carefully observed as the face.
Paint two people in a domestic interior. Let the physical space between them carry all of the emotional weight of the scene.
Create a portrait using a heavy impasto technique. Let the texture and the marks of the palette knife express something that smooth blending cannot.
Paint a figure in motion using a restricted palette of no more than three hues plus white. Color temperature does the work of form.
Create a large-scale portrait of someone you know well, from direct observation if at all possible.
Landscapes: Paint a landscape in the tradition of the Hudson River School — monumental scale, dramatic raking light, humanity made small.
Go outside and make a plein air study on location. Accept completely the challenges of changing light and imperfect conditions.
Paint a storm at sea as the Romantic painters understood it — nature as an overwhelming, indifferent, sublime force.
Create a moonlit night landscape using cold blue-violet shadow tones and the warmest possible light reserved for the moon itself.
Paint a winter landscape with primary attention to the subtle color variation within snow — the warm shadows, the cool highlights.
Create a landscape in which the sky occupies no less than three quarters of the total canvas area.
Paint a garden in peak midsummer — the controlled, pressurized wildness of everything growing at full capacity.
Paint the same view three times in three different amounts of detail: distant, middle, and close. Study what changes.
Both: Paint a figure in a landscape where the palette of their clothing deliberately, purposefully clashes with the surrounding environment.
Create a pastoral scene — a person completely at rest in nature — and treat stillness itself as your primary compositional challenge.
Paint a figure working outdoors in extreme or dramatic weather. Let the landscape appear completely indifferent to their effort.
Create a scene of outdoor celebration — a festival, a gathering, a moment of collective joy — exploring the relationship between happiness and open space.
Paint a single figure in a vast landscape. Determine the minimum size a figure can be before their humanity disappears entirely.
Create an allegory: a figure in a landscape where figure and setting together represent an abstract concept — grief, hope, time, or change.
Paint a large, populated outdoor scene — a market, a festival, a demonstration — where people are woven inseparably into a specific, recognizable place.
Create a large landscape with a single small figure positioned anywhere in the composition. Consider carefully how that placement changes everything.

People: Create a bold portrait in a pop art sensibility — flat color areas, strong black outlines, high contrast, and primary palette.
Paint a complete figure study using only palette knives. No brushes permitted at any stage of the work.
Create a portrait painted directly over a collaged layer of newspaper, found text, or torn magazine pages.
Paint a figure study using a palette inspired entirely by a specific time of day that you find emotionally resonant.
Create a portrait using a split-complementary color scheme — choose one primary and the two colors flanking its complement.
Paint a figure that is in the process of merging into or emerging from a highly graphic, repeating patterned background.
Create a portrait with the graphic sensibility of 1970s design — flat color shapes, warm earthy palette, bold simplified forms.
Paint four small rapid portraits in one session, spending no more than twenty minutes on each. Commit to speed over refinement.
Landscapes: Create a vivid, saturated landscape in colors from the opposite of reality — a deep purple sky, a red sea, a yellow mountain.
Paint an entire landscape using only a palette knife. No brushes at any point. Texture is now your primary descriptive tool.
Create a night city scene using neon accent colors pushing against a near-black background — sign light, window light, reflections.
Build a landscape using masking tape to create hard, clean geometric divisions between sky, land, and water.
Create an abstract interpretation of a real place you know well — keep the geography, abandon the realistic color.
Paint a landscape that reads completely differently at arm's length than it does from across the room.
Create a triptych: three same-sized panels showing the identical view in three different seasons.
Paint a landscape with a deliberately graphic, poster-like quality — simplified silhouettes, strong horizon, bold flat shapes.
Both: Paint a street scene using bold, flattened graphic shapes. Simplify both people and buildings until they nearly become abstraction.
Create a mural-scale composition — even if small in actual dimension — depicting a figure or group in a place that is personally meaningful.
Paint an outdoor festival or celebration scene in vivid, high-chroma color. Embrace the chaos of many people in an open outdoor space.
Create a composition where the pattern or texture of a figure's clothing continues visually and seamlessly into the landscape behind them.
Paint a mythological or folkloric figure placed specifically and incongruously in a real, contemporary, recognizable landscape.
Create a painting that begins with precise representation at its center and becomes increasingly abstract toward every edge.
Paint a transit waiting space — a bus stop, train platform, or ferry queue — focusing on ordinary people in ordinary in-between time.
Create a bright, graphic illustration of a folk tale or childhood story using the full chromatic range of your palette.

People: Draw a complete portrait using only cross-hatching — no outlines at any point. Every edge and every form must be built entirely from intersecting lines.
Create a portrait using a single continuous line. The pen never leaves the paper from the first mark to the last.
Illustrate a figure in active motion using only ink splatter, flicks, and fast gestural marks. Deliberate accidents only.
Draw a crowded scene where every figure exists at a different dilution of ink — densest black in the foreground, palest wash in the distance.
Create five small portraits of five different faces using nothing but stippling — dots of varying density building every form.
Draw a figure using negative space as your primary tool — the ink defines everything surrounding the figure, and the figure itself remains as bare paper.
Illustrate a person's emotional state using entirely non-representational ink marks. No recognizable figure. Pure mark-making.
Create a portrait using large, committed brushstrokes in the spirit of sumi-e. No corrections. No hesitation. First marks are final marks.
Landscapes: Draw a dense forest using only vertical lines. Let the closeness of the lines control how dark each section becomes.
Create a seascape built from horizontal ink strokes of varying weight and varying spacing. Nothing else in the composition.
Draw a city skyline at night using ink wash — build the atmospheric glow from multiple layers of increasingly diluted ink.
Illustrate a mountain landscape using only stippling. Every shadow, every gradient, every surface — nothing but dots.
Draw a stormy sky using the broadest, most aggressive brushstrokes you are willing to make.
Create a landscape where the foreground is rendered in the most obsessive detail you can manage and the background is almost completely bare.
Draw a river or waterfall using only flowing, calligraphic lines. The line quality itself should feel like moving water.
Create a night scene using ink wash where the white of the paper is the absolute brightest point and undiluted black ink is used only for the single darkest accent.
Both: Draw a figure on a bridge above a city. Use sharp, precise lines for the figure and loose ink wash for everything beneath and around them.
Create an illustration in the tradition of classic pen-and-ink book illustrations: one figure in one richly detailed environment.
Draw a scene from folklore or mythology using bold, confident brushwork. No tentative marks.
Create a single comics panel — one isolated moment — and make every compositional and mark-making choice serve the story.
Draw a figure moving through a landscape that communicates unfamiliarity and strangeness — somewhere the figure clearly does not belong.
Illustrate the feeling of a specific piece of music or a specific poem: one figure in one landscape that captures the emotional register of the words or sounds.
Create a wide panoramic landscape with figures rendered as pure silhouette in the foreground, ink wash handling the entire sky.
Draw a market, bazaar, or street food scene: dense cross-hatching for the shadows under awnings, ink wash for the open-air atmosphere.

People: Create a detailed character sprite for a game that does not exist. Include at minimum an idle, a walking cycle, and one action frame.
Design a pixel portrait of a specific person using a palette restricted to exactly sixteen colors.
Create a set of pixel emotion icons using the same face — eight different emotional states from the same base character.
Design a fantasy RPG character class of your own invention. The silhouette alone should communicate the role.
Create the same portrait at three sizes — 16x16, 32x32, and 64x64 pixels. Observe how complexity and expressiveness change at each scale.
Design a villain whose visual design makes their core motivation immediately readable without any text or context.
Create a complete pixel character sheet — front view, back view, side view, and three distinct facial expressions.
Design a non-human character — a construct, a creature, a spirit — that nonetheless communicates immediately recognizable and relatable emotion.
Landscapes: Create a pixel landscape at three different times of day in three equal panels. Let only the palette change — not the composition.
Design a single-screen game environment that tells a complete story about who lives there without any characters present.
Create a pixel seascape with animated water — sketch the keyframes of a three-frame looping animation.
Design a fantasy biome in pixel art where the palette itself communicates the climate, the danger level, and the dominant weather.
Create a dungeon room using tile-based logic — design a space that would be interesting to navigate and that rewards exploration.
Design the same sky at four distinct times of day as four isolated horizontal strips. Study how palette drives atmosphere entirely.
Create a pixel cityscape — future, historical, or alternate — where the era is completely legible in the architectural details.
Design an outdoor environment from a specific real decade in history. Make the time period identifiable in every visual detail.
Both: Create a single-screen pixel scene in which one character in one environment together tell a complete wordless story.
Design a title screen for an imaginary game — character, setting, title treatment, and overall mood in a single unified composition.
Create a pixel diorama: a character placed within a home environment that reflects their personality in every furnishing and detail.
Design a single story beat as a cutscene panel — the most pivotal moment in an imaginary narrative, expressed through composition alone.
Create a pixel art recreation or reinterpretation of a famous painting, translated fully into pixel-native visual language.
Design a small overworld map icon: a distinct figure standing in or before a distinct environment type.
Create a pixel animation key frame — a figure in a landscape at a single moment of high dramatic tension.
Design an NPC character and their home environment as a single cohesive visual package — the space and the person should feel inseparable.

People: Create a portrait that combines printed or cut photographic fragments with hand-drawn or hand-painted elements. Let the seam between them be visible.
Build a figure from torn paper, found textures, and ink on a mixed surface. The construction process should be part of what the viewer sees.
Create a portrait in which the background is composed entirely of handwritten text — journal entries, letters, lists — relevant to the subject.
Combine watercolor washes for the large forms with gel pen detail work for the fine marks. Let the contrast between wet and precise be your primary texture.
Create a figure using fabric scraps, thread, and paint on a mixed substrate. The figure can be sewn as much as drawn or painted.
Build a portrait where the subject is painted or drawn and the surrounding background is constructed from maps, letters, printed ephemera, or found documents.
Combine relief printing or rubber stamp textures as an underlay with painted or drawn figures worked over the top.
Create a self-portrait using only whatever materials are physically present in your immediate space right now. Quantity and variety are not the point.
Landscapes: Create a landscape using hand-painted paper that you then cut into depth planes and layer physically over one another.
Build a landscape incorporating actual collected natural materials — pressed leaves, dried soil, bark, sand — fixed permanently to the surface.
Create a topographic landscape by layering torn and folded paper into physical raised contour ridges across the surface.
Combine a printed photograph with hand-painted or drawn additions and subtractions to create something the original photograph never was.
Build a seascape incorporating actual sand, salt crystals, or translucent layers of medium to create physical surface depth.
Create a cityscape collage assembled entirely from architectural fragments cut from magazines, maps, technical drawings, and printed documents.
Layer tissue paper, ink wash, and deliberate mark-making to create a landscape of pure atmospheric mood without literal description.
Build a landscape from entirely unexpected materials — packaging, labels, receipts, ticket stubs — and discover what world they want to become.
Both: Create a scene in which the figure is painted or drawn in a realistic mode and the landscape surrounding them is built entirely from collage.
Build a narrative image — one person in one place — where every distinct element is created from a different material or technique.
Create a response to a specific piece of music you are listening to right now. Let the figure and the landscape both be shaped by what you hear.
Combine printmaking with painting: ink a textured surface and print it as your ground layer, then paint or draw figures directly over the printed texture.
Build a portrait or landscape that is materially of a specific decade — use actual ephemera from that era as your primary building material.
Create a mythological or folkloric scene using mixed media. Choose materials that comment on or deepen the meaning of the story being depicted.
Combine embroidery or hand-stitching with paint or ink. Let thread become line, let fabric become ground, let stitch become mark.
Create an image with a hidden layer — something concealed within or beneath the visible materials that only close physical examination will reveal.
